planting.note: Divide established clumps carefully in early spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Hungarian acanthus has thick, fleshy roots that break easily and each fragment regenerates into a new plant — making it very persistent once established. It is more cold-hardy than A. mollis (to zone 4) and tolerates drier, more alkaline soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Hungarian acanthus is a Balkan native and the most cold-hardy commonly available acanthus — surviving to zone 4 where the more popular A. mollis would perish — with the same fundamental character: enormous, deeply pinnately lobed, shining, dark-green leaves of extraordinary architectural quality (the leaf form that inspired the capital decoration of Corinthian architectural columns in ancient Greece) and tall spikes of hooded purple-and-white flowers in summer. The leaves of A. hungaricus are more deeply and finely cut than those of A. mollis — approaching the quality of deeply incised architectural decorations — and are slightly smaller but not less impressive. Like A. mollis, once planted it is essentially permanent due to its thick, regenerating roots, and can be difficult to remove. Outstanding as an architectural accent plant in the border where bold structural foliage through the entire growing season is more important than flower color. Tolerates more alkaline soil and more drought than A. mollis. The winter-persistent seed heads provide structural interest.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; agave-leaf sea holly is evergreen in mild winters and forms an impressive, large rosette of leaves that requires substantial space. The flower stems are summer-produced on previous year's growth, and the spent stems should be cut back after seeding. More cold-tolerant than expected for an Argentine species.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; agave-leaf sea holly is a large-scale Argentine eryngium that earns its common name through its remarkable foliage — the basal leaves are broad, strap-shaped, toothed along the margin with spine-tipped teeth, and arranged in a bold rosette that closely resembles a medium-sized agave in form and architecture. This agave-like rosette, in glossy, bright green, provides architectural structure throughout the year. In summer, tall, branching flower scapes emerge from the center carrying numerous small, pale-green to whitish, typical eryngium flower heads (each a rounded bur of spiky bracts above a domed disc) — smaller and less colorful than the metallic-blue European sea hollies but collectively impressive for their number and the height of the scapes. The whole plant creates a bold, architectural structure in the garden — a rosette of agave-like leaves at ground level sending up flowering stalks to 5–6 feet. Outstanding as an architectural specimen or back-of-border plant in drought-tolerant, Mediterranean-climate, or prairie-style gardens.

planting.note: Divide established clumps after flowering in late spring (when the foliage begins to die back) or plant container-grown plants in fall; South African bulbinella grows in winter and flowers in late winter through spring (blooming when the South African Cape experiences its cool, moist season), then goes summer dormant. It requires moist conditions during the growing season and dryness during summer dormancy.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; South African bulbinella is a bold, cool-season-active perennial from the Cape mountains of South Africa, producing tufts of bright-green, strap-shaped or broader, succulent-tinged leaves that emerge in autumn, grow through winter, and in late winter through spring send up upright, dense, bottlebrush-like spikes of vivid, golden-yellow, star-shaped flowers — each spike 12–18 inches long, packed with hundreds of individual yellow blooms creating a dense cylindrical shape similar to a yellow bottlebrush or a very fat, yellow cat's tail. The bloom coincides with the Cape fynbos wet season and represents a flash of intense yellow color in the late-winter to spring garden at a time when few other bold yellow flowers are available. After flowering and seed-set in early summer, the plant goes completely summer dormant, disappearing until autumn rains trigger new growth. Outstanding for Mediterranean-climate gardens and zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens where the seasonal rhythm of cool-wet/hot-dry matches the plant's natural South African Cape requirements.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; broad-leaved bulbine is more cold-tolerant and larger than the commonly grown orange-flowered B. frutescens. The gel from the fresh leaves is used in South African traditional medicine similarly to aloe vera — for burns, skin irritation, and minor wounds. The leaves are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; broad-leaved bulbine is a larger South African species than the orange-flowered B. frutescens — producing broader, more succulent, aloe-like strap leaves in rosettes and in spring through summer, tall, branching scapes of small, yellow, six-petaled, star-shaped flowers with distinctive, fluffy, bearded yellow stamens that give each individual flower a charming, hairy-bearded appearance. The multiple scapes on an established clump produce a fine, airy cloud of small yellow flowers over the striking succulent foliage for an extended period. In South African traditional medicine, the clear, viscous gel from the leaves is used topically for burns, itching, and skin irritations, similarly to aloe vera but with a more watery, less viscous consistency. As a garden plant it is a low-maintenance, drought-tolerant succulent perennial for hot, dry, sunny positions, combining well with other South African succulent plants — kniphofia, bulbinella, agapanthus, and ornamental grasses — in water-wise subtropical plantings.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 8–12 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, sandy to gravelly, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; desert lily grows at extraordinary depth in nature (bulbs found as deep as 20 inches) to escape summer heat and drought. It requires the hot, dry desert summer dormancy of the Sonoran Desert and is not suitable for areas with wet summers. In its native habitat it flowers only in years with sufficient winter rainfall.

directions: Plant bulbs very deep in very well-drained, sandy to gravelly, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; desert lily is a remarkable Sonoran Desert native — the only species in its genus — that produces one of the most beautiful and improbable flower displays of the North American desert: slender, upright racemes of 2-inch, pure-white, fragrant, funnel-shaped lily flowers with a pale green stripe down each petal's reverse, appearing in February through April in the sandy washes and desert floors of the Sonoran Desert from southern California to Arizona and Baja California. The plants emerge only when sufficient winter rainfall triggers their growth, disappearing completely in dry years and producing spectacular flowering displays in wet years — creating one of the variable miracles of the desert spring wildflower season. The deep-burrowing bulbs (planted naturally at 12–20 inch depths by small animals and by the force of the plant's contractile roots) are one of the deepest-reaching of all desert bulbs, providing insulation from summer heat. In cultivation it requires the precise conditions of its native habitat — deep, well-drained, sandy, alkaline soil, dry summers, and occasional winter watering to simulate desert winter rain. A collector's plant of special botanical and ecological significance.
